source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, AI, global warming, geopolitics
summary: Scaled foundation model companies are dramatically better investments post-reasoning models due to emergence of data flywheel
summary_extended: Baker argues that the emergence of reasoning models (via RLVF and test-time compute scaling) has fundamentally transformed the investment case for scaled foundation model companies by creating a user-data-product flywheel that did not previously exist in standard LLMs. Specifically, he contends that a large user base now feeds back into product quality improvement, meaning companies with the most users compound their model advantage over time — a structural moat that was absent before reasoning models. Baker identifies OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI, and Google DeepMind as the primary beneficiaries of this dynamic, viewing them as a durable oligopoly that can now be valued on revenue multiples rather than purely speculative terms. The investment implication is to favor these scaled foundation model incumbents over challengers, as the flywheel makes it increasingly difficult for new entrants to close the gap. Notably, most of the named beneficiaries (OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI) are private, limiting direct public-market expression of this thesis primarily to Alphabet via Google DeepMind.
detail: Reasoning models (RLVF, test-time compute) created a user-data-product flywheel that previously did not exist in LLMs. Now having a large user base feeds back into product quality, making OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI, and Google DeepMind structurally advantaged. Baker views these as now-valueable on revenue multiples and likely to form a durable oligopoly.
confidence_evidence: Baker explicitly states 'these scaled foundation model companies are much better investments post-reasoning' and elaborates the structural mechanism at length
quote_fragment: scaled foundation model companies are much better investments post-reasoning

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google DeepMind is explicitly named by Baker as one of the four scaled foundation model companies forming the durable oligopoly, and Alphabet is the only publicly listed entity among them.

META was implied from the broader transcript context but Baker's thesis specifically names OpenAI, Anthropic, XAI, and Google DeepMind as the oligopoly. Meta's LLaMA is an open-weight model and is not part of the closed user-data flywheel thesis Baker describes; Meta is not named or structurally implied here.

Microsoft has an exclusive cloud partnership and significant equity stake in OpenAI, the primary named beneficiary of this thesis. As OpenAI's commercial distribution arm and largest investor, MSFT is a direct public-market proxy for OpenAI's flywheel advantage, making it a genuine direct beneficiary rather than tangential.
